description:
<title>Symlink Management & 404-Error Summary for Archived Web Logs</title>

    <description>
        You are acting as a log analyst who has been handed a directory full of archived
        Apache access logs.  Your objectives are to organise these logs with symbolic
        links, extract a specific metric (HTTP 404 occurrences) and publish the results
        in a strictly-formatted report.

        The following archive hierarchy already exists on the filesystem:

          /home/user/archive_logs/2023-07-01/access.log
          /home/user/archive_logs/2023-07-02/access.log
          /home/user/archive_logs/2023-07-03/access.log

        Step-by-step requirements:

        1.  Create a new working directory called
                /home/user/log_analysis
            if it does not already exist.

        2.  Inside that directory, create a sub-directory named
                /home/user/log_analysis/links

        3.  Inside the links/ directory, create three symbolic links that point to the
            original archived log files.  They must be named **exactly** like this:

                /home/user/log_analysis/links/2023-07-01.access.log  ➜ /home/user/archive_logs/2023-07-01/access.log
                /home/user/log_analysis/links/2023-07-02.access.log  ➜ /home/user/archive_logs/2023-07-02/access.log
                /home/user/log_analysis/links/2023-07-03.access.log  ➜ /home/user/archive_logs/2023-07-03/access.log

            (Use relative or absolute paths—your choice—but the links themselves must live
             under the links/ directory and must resolve without errors.)

        4.  Parse each of the three linked files and count how many lines contain the
            HTTP status code 404.  (In standard Apache logs, the status code is the first
            numeric field after the quoted request; e.g. `"GET / HTTP/1.1" 404 512`.)

        5.  Produce a plain-text summary file at
                /home/user/log_analysis/report_404_summary.txt
            with the **exact** layout shown below.  The automated grader performs a
            byte-level comparison, so follow the template precisely:

                DATE|TOTAL_404_HITS
                2023-07-01|<count>
                2023-07-02|<count>
                2023-07-03|<count>

            • The first line must be the literal header  
              `DATE|TOTAL_404_HITS` (all caps, no leading/trailing spaces).  
            • Each subsequent line must list the date, a single vertical bar (`|`), and
              the integer count with **no extra spaces**.  
            • Lines must be separated by a single UNIX newline (`\n`) and the file must
              end with exactly one trailing newline.

        6.  For convenience, create a **symbolic link**
                /home/user/latest_analysis
            that points to `/home/user/log_analysis/report_404_summary.txt`.

        7.  Double-check that:
            • All symlinks are valid and resolve to existing targets.  
            • The summary file strictly follows the required format.  
            • The latest_analysis symlink resolves correctly.

        You are free to use any command-line utilities available in a standard Linux
        environment (e.g. ln, grep, awk, wc, etc.).  Produce console output showing your
        progress if you like, but the key deliverables are the symlinks and the summary
        file placed in the correct locations with the exact specified content.
    </description>
name: task_000000_cae850ca
